_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Patients recovering reasonably well in the postoperative period suddenly complain of pink or brownish colored serosanguinous discharge (pathognomonic sign)
Occurs usually on the 6th to 8th postoperative day
If skin sutures are removed, omentum or small bowel coils will be seen outside

ppd_preauth.q: Did clinical presentation/photograph confirm the diagnosis?
Was clinical presentation indicative of surgery?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and treatment details?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advise at the time of discharge?
Did the burst abdomen occur within 3-4 weeks after surgery?

common_queries: Clinical presentation or photograph not indicative of surgery for burst abdomen.
Burst abdomen reported outside the 3-4 weeks post-surgery window (as per TMS pop-up criteria).
Clinical notes are missing or lack detailed history, signs & symptoms, planned line of treatment, or indication for procedure.
Clinical photograph is missing or does not confirm the diagnosis.
Detailed Indoor Case Papers (ICPs) are incomplete or missing daily vitals and treatment details.
Detailed Procedure & operative notes are missing or do not describe the surgical steps.

clinical_key_pointers: A soundly healed abdominal scar can withstand any amount of intra-abdominal pressure. However, 1-2% of the abdominal wounds (incisions) give way within 2 weeks of surgery, resulting in prolapse of intraabdominal contents outside.
Causative agents are multifactorial, making it not possible to prevent wound dehiscence totally.
Factors responsible for wound dehiscence: Surgery, Sepsis, Suture material used, Surgeon-related factors, Sick patient, Straining, Poor nutritional status post-surgery.
Treatment involves reassurance, covering the bowel or contents with pads and bandage, and emergency surgery and closure if indicated.
contraindications: Early closure of wound dehiscence should NOT be attempted if the cause is increased intra-abdominal pressure.
Delayed closure helps in reducing oedema and safe closure.
Sometimes, use of a Vac or open management followed by skin grafting may be more beneficial.
